description: Iconic páramo plant known for its distinctive rosette form and woolly leaves that trap moisture from clouds. Can live up to 150 years and grows only a few centimeters per year.
A vulnerable frailejón species of the Cordillera Central with distinctive silver-grey woolly leaves, forming dense colonies in high-elevation wetlands and grasslands above 3400m.
A leguminous shrub endemic to the Eastern Cordillera of Colombia, producing striking blue-purple flower spikes and fixing atmospheric nitrogen in nutrient-poor páramo soils.
A resinous shrub common across Andean páramos, producing small yellow flowers and forming dense thickets along stream margins; used in traditional medicine and resistant to light burning.
A robust páramo shrub in the Asteraceae family with small daisy-like white flowers, forming extensive shrublands at transitional zones between subpáramo and open grasslands.
A cushion-forming plantain that creates dense star-shaped mats over boggy páramo terrain, playing a critical role in water retention and providing microhabitat for invertebrates and amphibians.
The dominant matrix grass of Colombian páramo ecosystems, forming extensive yellow-gold grasslands that characterize the landscape and provide essential structure for the entire food web.
A vulnerable sedge named in honor of botanist Antoine Cleef, forming compact cushions in waterlogged peatlands and considered a key indicator species for pristine high-Andean wetlands.

An endangered everlasting plant with dense white flowering heads, restricted to undisturbed high-elevation peatlands and considered highly sensitive to hydrological alterations caused by drainage and agriculture.

A large bromeliad that flowers spectacularly once before dying, with tall spike inflorescences reaching 3m, providing critical nectar resources for hummingbirds and insects in high-Andean communities.

A critically endangered harlequin toad endemic to the páramos of the Eastern Cordillera, not recorded for decades and feared extinct until its rediscovery in 2006, now severely threatened by chytridiomycosis fungal disease and habitat loss.

The woolly tapir is the only tapir species adapted to cold high-Andean environments, acting as a crucial seed disperser in páramo and cloud forest ecosystems; populations are severely fragmented by habitat loss.

South America's only bear and a keystone ecosystem engineer, the spectacled bear forages across cloud forest and páramo zones and disperses seeds of crucial high-Andean plant species across large territories.

The Andean condor is the world's largest flying bird by wingspan, serving as a cultural symbol of Colombia and an apex scavenger whose populations crashed due to persecution and lead poisoning from carcasses shot with lead ammunition.

Colombia's critically endangered woolly monkey is restricted to cloud forest below páramo zones in the Central and Western cordilleras, devastated by hunting and deforestation with fewer than 1,000 individuals estimated to remain.

A striking cloud forest toucan that forages along the upper tree line and subpáramo margins, serving as an important disperser of large-seeded cloud forest trees and acting as an indicator of forest integrity.

A small insectivorous marsupial historically recorded in Colombian Andean foothills, now classified as endangered due to extensive deforestation and agricultural expansion that has eliminated most of its suitable forest habitat.

A cold-adapted anole lizard endemic to the high Andes of Colombia, physiologically specialized to tolerate freezing temperatures through reduced metabolic rates and found only in intact páramo grasslands and rocky outcrops.

A secretive ground-dwelling antpitta found in cloud forest and subpáramo edges, known for its loud far-carrying calls at dawn and dusk; its presence indicates forest quality and continuity between elevation zones.

The tapeti or forest cottontail is a small rabbit distributed across Colombian Andean ecosystems from cloud forest to subpáramo, playing an important role as prey for Andean foxes and raptors throughout the high-elevation food web.
